interactions.summary: Jiang finishes the Turnbull passage about a man found sleeping during Malimo singing being killed, buried, and never mentioned again, then asks why sleeping is worse than killing in that world.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading quoted material and posing a classroom question.

interactions.summary: Jiang builds the classroom analogy: a class exists only through collective participation, so a sleeper says the ritual is not real and commits violence against the community, religion, forest, and god.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang leads; student responses are partial.

interactions.summary: Jiang names the power of religion and religious imagination: collective belief can make something more real than reality, unlike modern materialism that denies what cannot be measured.
Jiang reads Turnbull's account of repeated evening Malimo performances, where Turnbull describes the ritual as pretending and make-believe while sensing something real beneath it.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading quoted material from Turnbull.

interactions.summary: Jiang asks students to find the insulting outsider word, identifies 'pretending,' and argues that for believers the imagined religious world is more true, powerful, and real than the visible world.

speaker_notes.note: These segments contain quoted Turnbull material embedded in Jiang's analysis.
Student answers are fragmentary; Jiang's classroom analogy and the target word 'pretending' are clear.

claims.claim: In the Malimo ritual world Jiang describes, the greatest crime is not killing someone but sleeping during the religion.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's interpretation of quoted Turnbull material.

claims.claim: Jiang's classroom analogy says a collective ritual exists only when everyone participates in it; a sleeper denies the collective experience is real.
Nonparticipation is treated as violence against the community, the religion, the forest, and the god because it says the shared ritual world does not matter.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's interpretation of the Malimo punishment logic.

claims.claim: Jiang says religion can be more real than reality as long as everyone believes in it, and nonbelief threatens the religion's survival.
He contrasts most human history as spiritual with the modern materialistic view that what cannot be seen, measured, or located is not real.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture contrast between spiritual and materialistic worldviews.

claims.claim: Jiang rejects materialism's dismissal of imagination, arguing that imagination cannot coherently be denied just because it cannot be measured like a physical object.
Jiang says Turnbull's repeated use of 'pretending' marks an outsider misunderstanding because the Pygmies would regard the ritual as true, serious, and real.
The religious imagination can make an imagined world more true, more powerful, and more real than the visible world when many people imagine it together.
claims.temporal_scope: Core model claim in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: The lecture's provocation: in this world, the worst crime is not murder but sleeping through religion.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the greatest crime you can commit is not to kill someone but to sleep during the religion
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This shocking reversal forces the audience to enter the logic of collective sacred reality.

signature_moments.moment: A sleeper commits violence not against one person but against the community, religion, forest, and god.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you're committing violence on the community... insulting the forest... insulting the god
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why disbelief is treated as existential danger, not private opinion.

signature_moments.moment: Religion becomes more real than reality when everyone believes it is true.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's title concept in its most compact form.

signature_moments.moment: A nonbeliever may have to die because disbelief can make the religion die.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if he doesn't believe in it then it might cause our religion to die
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It exposes the brutal social logic behind sacred consensus.

signature_moments.moment: Pretending is the outsider's insult; for believers, the imagined world is true.
signature_moments.source_phrase: We think they're pretending... They think it's all true
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It crystallizes the gulf between materialist observation and religious imagination.

signature_moments.moment: Many people imagining together can make a world more true, powerful, and real than this world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: as long as many of you are imagining it together, it's more true, more powerful, more real
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's social theory of religious world-making in one sentence.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A reality that exists through shared participation, like Jiang's classroom analogy for Malimo ritual.
The human power to imagine a shared sacred world that becomes more real and authoritative than visible reality.
The modern view Jiang defines as treating what cannot be seen, measured, or felt as unreal.
The outsider word Jiang says insults the ritual because it assumes make-believe where believers see truth.
The view Jiang attributes to most of human history: spirits and souls exist and matter as much as or more than visible reality.

uncertainty_notes.note: The classroom dialogue is partly noisy; the public read should use Jiang's analogy rather than quote unstable student responses.
The quoted passage contains repeated 'pretending' language from Turnbull; attribution matters because Jiang is analyzing and challenging that word.
The transcript has several false starts and repeated classroom prompts while Jiang searches for the word 'pretending.'
